questions.question:
An unrestricted use of the "go to" statement is harmful because of which of the following reason(s):
A. It makes it more difficult to verify programs.
B. It makes programs more inefficient.
C. It results in the compiler generating longer programs.
D. It results in the compiler generating longer machine code.
questions.answer: A. It makes it more difficult to verify programs.
questions.to_find: Which reason(s) make unrestricted use of 'go to' harmful?

understand.plain: The 'go to' statement allows a program to jump to any labeled location unconditionally. When used without restriction, the control flow of the program becomes tangled, making it very hard to follow logic, prove correctness, or debug. This is the core of the famous 'Goto Considered Harmful' argument by Dijkstra.

keywords.explain: An unconditional jump to a labeled point in the program, bypassing normal sequential or structured control flow.
The process of formally or informally proving that a program is correct. Unrestricted jumps break the ability to reason about pre- and post-conditions at each program point.
keywords.example: If a loop can be entered from multiple goto statements, invariants become nearly impossible to establish.

keywords.explain: A paradigm using sequential, selection (if/else), and iteration (loops) constructs, avoiding goto, to keep control flow clear and verifiable.
keywords.example: Using while/for loops instead of goto-based loops.

understand.visual_alt: A flowchart showing tangled control flow arrows caused by unrestricted goto jumps, making the flow unreadable compared to a clean structured flowchart.

given.aim: Identify the correct reason why unrestricted goto use is considered harmful.

terms.meaning: Unconditional jump instruction in a program
terms.connects: The harm arises from what goto does to control flow reasoning

terms.meaning: Proving that a program produces correct outputs for all valid inputs
terms.example: Hoare logic uses pre/post conditions at each statement
terms.connects: Goto breaks sequential reasoning, making verification very hard

terms.meaning: How compact and fast the generated binary code is
terms.example: A compiler may optimize loops better than goto-based jumps
terms.connects: goto does NOT necessarily make machine code longer or programs more inefficient

given.plan: Evaluate each option: A talks about verification difficulty (true and the primary academic argument), B about efficiency (not inherently true), C and D about code size (not directly caused by goto). The accepted answer is A.

steps.title: Evaluate Option A — Verification difficulty
steps.apply: Dijkstra's 1968 paper 'Go To Statement Considered Harmful' argues that goto creates arbitrary control flow paths, breaking the ability to associate a program's textual position with a known runtime state. This makes formal and informal verification extremely difficult.
steps.note: This is the primary and well-established reason. Verification requires that at each point in the code, the set of possible program states is bounded and reasoned about — goto violates this.

steps.title: Evaluate Options B, C, D
steps.apply: B: goto does not inherently make programs inefficient — a simple jump may be very fast. C and D: The compiler generates code based on semantics; goto itself does not produce longer programs or longer machine code. These are distractors.
steps.note: Options B, C, D are incorrect. Only A correctly captures why goto is considered harmful in computer science theory.

solution.result: A. It makes it more difficult to verify programs.

questions.formula_note: Dijkstra's 'Goto Considered Harmful' principle: unrestricted goto destroys structured control flow and makes program verification intractable.

questions.question:
An unrestricted use of the "goto" statement is harmful because
A. It makes it more difficult to verify programs.
B. It increases the running time of the programs.
C. It increases the memory required for the programs.
D. It results in the compiler generating longer machine code.
questions.answer: A. It makes it more difficult to verify programs.
questions.to_find: Which of the given reasons correctly explains why unrestricted goto is harmful?

understand.plain: This is essentially the same conceptual question as GATE 1989 — the 'goto' statement is harmful primarily because unrestricted jumps destroy the structured flow of a program, making it very difficult to reason about, test, and formally verify the program's correctness. The other options (runtime, memory, machine code size) are not genuine consequences of goto.

keywords.explain: A low-level jump instruction that transfers control unconditionally to a labeled line, bypassing structured constructs.
Establishing that a program meets its specification, either by testing or formal proof. Goto creates spaghetti code that breaks proof-based reasoning.
keywords.example: Invariants at a loop body cannot be established if arbitrary gotos enter the loop from outside.

understand.visual_alt: Spaghetti code diagram showing multiple goto arrows jumping across code blocks in unpredictable ways.

given.aim: Identify the correct reason why unrestricted goto is harmful.

terms.meaning: Using goto freely to jump anywhere in code without discipline
terms.example: Jumping into the middle of a loop from outside it

terms.meaning: How long a program takes to execute
terms.connects: goto does not inherently increase running time — a single jump is O(1)

terms.meaning: How much RAM the program needs during execution
terms.connects: goto has no significant effect on memory usage

given.plan: Rule out options B (runtime), C (memory), D (machine code size) as they are not true effects of goto. Accept A as the standard textbook answer.

steps.title: Check each option against known theory
steps.apply:
A: Correct — formal verification (e.g., using Floyd-Hoare logic) requires knowing the set of reachable states at each program point; arbitrary gotos make this undecidable in general.
B: Incorrect — goto is a single branch instruction, no slower than an if-statement branch.
C: Incorrect — goto does not allocate or increase memory usage.
D: Incorrect — the compiler produces code based on what the program does; goto itself is just a jump instruction, not a code-size inflator.
steps.note: All major programming languages textbooks and the original Dijkstra paper point to verification difficulty as the core harm of goto.

steps.title: Select the answer
steps.apply: Only option A correctly identifies the real harm: it is very hard to verify programs with arbitrary goto because the control flow graph becomes unstructured and reasoning about invariants breaks down.
steps.note: This question appears nearly identically in GATE 1989 (Q 9.4.1) with the same answer A.

solution.result: A. It makes it more difficult to verify programs.

questions.formula_note: Structured programming theorem: any computable function can be written without goto using sequence, selection, and iteration — goto adds no power but makes verification much harder.

questions.to_find: Values of W, X, Y, Z after execution, and any overflow/underflow errors.

understand.plain: This program uses unsigned byte variables (range 0-255) and one unsigned integer variable Z (range 0-65535 or larger). When arithmetic overflows an unsigned byte, it wraps around modulo 256. When a subtraction underflows below 0 for an unsigned type, it is either an error or wraps. Here: W = 255+1 overflows byte to 0, X = 255 fits, Y = 1-255 underflows (error), Z = 255+1 = 256 fits in unsigned integer.

keywords.explain: An 8-bit integer storing values 0 to 255. Arithmetic that goes above 255 overflows (wraps to 0 modulo 256) or raises an error depending on the language.
A multi-byte integer (typically 16 or 32 bits) that can hold larger positive values, e.g. 0 to 65535 for 16-bit.
keywords.example: 256 fits in unsigned integer but not in unsigned byte.

keywords.explain: Overflow: result exceeds the maximum value of the type. Underflow: result is below the minimum (0 for unsigned). Both are error conditions.
keywords.example: unsigned byte: 255+1 -> overflow (result 0 or error); 1-255 -> underflow (error)

= 0</text><rect x="150" y="80" width="120" height="60" rx="6" class="bg"/><text x="210" y="103" class="lbl ink" text-anchor="middle">X = A</text><text x="210" y="122" class="sub mut" text-anchor="middle">= 255</text><text x="210" y="137" class="tag ok" text-anchor="middle">OK</text><rect x="290" y="80" width="120" height="60" rx="6" class="hi"/><text x="350" y="103" class="lbl ink" text-anchor="middle">Y = B-A</text><text x="350" y="122" class="sub mut" text-anchor="middle">1-255 = -254</text><text x="350" y="137" class="tag no" text-anchor="middle">underflow! error</text><rect x="430" y="80" width="120" height="60" rx="6" class="bg"/><text x="490" y="103" class="lbl ink" text-anchor="middle">Z = A+B</text><text x="490" y="122" class="sub mut" text-anchor="middle">= 256</text><text x="490" y="137" class="tag ok" text-anchor="middle">OK (uint)</text><text x="280" y="200" class="lbl ink" text-anchor="middle">W=0, X=255, Y=error, Z=256</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Table showing each variable: W = A+B = 256 overflows unsigned byte to 0; X = A = 255 OK; Y = B-A = 1-255 underflows (error); Z = A+B = 256 fits in unsigned integer (OK).

given.aim: Compute the values of W, X, Y, Z given the assignments and variable types.

terms.meaning: Initial values assigned to unsigned byte variables
terms.example: A is at maximum value for a byte (2^8 - 1 = 255)
terms.connects: Adding 1 to A causes overflow; subtracting A from B causes underflow

terms.meaning: 255 + 1 = 256, but 256 cannot be stored in unsigned byte (max 255)
terms.example: 256 mod 256 = 0, so W = 0 (overflow wrap) or error

terms.meaning: 1 - 255 = -254, which is negative and cannot be stored in unsigned byte
terms.example: Unsigned types cannot represent negative values

given.plan: Evaluate each assignment with the type constraints: unsigned byte holds 0-255, unsigned integer holds larger values. Check for overflow (result > 255 in byte) or underflow (result < 0 in unsigned byte).

steps.title: Compute W = A + B in unsigned byte
steps.apply: A = 255, B = 1. A + B = 256. Since 256 > 255 (max for unsigned byte), overflow occurs. In most implementations, W = 256 mod 256 = 0. The overflow is an error condition.
steps.note: W = 0 with an overflow/error flag. The question accepts 0 as the stored value.

steps.title: Compute X = A in unsigned byte
steps.apply: X := A = 255. No overflow or underflow. 255 fits in unsigned byte perfectly.

steps.title: Compute Y = B - A in unsigned byte
steps.apply: Y := B - A = 1 - 255 = -254. Since Y is unsigned byte and cannot hold negative values, this is an underflow/error condition.
steps.note: Y = error (underflow). The question marks this as an error, not a wrapped value.

steps.title: Compute Z = A + B in unsigned integer
steps.apply: Z := A + B = 255 + 1 = 256. Z is an unsigned integer (at least 16 bits, range >= 65535), so 256 fits without overflow.

solution.result: W = 0 (overflow error), X = 255, Y = error (underflow), Z = 256. Answer: A. 0, 255, error, 256.

questions.formula_note: Unsigned integer overflow: result mod 2^n; unsigned underflow (negative result) is an error for unsigned types. Key concept: unsigned byte range is 0-255, unsigned integer range is larger (e.g., 0-65535 for 16-bit).

questions.to_find: What does writeln(result) print when f uses call-by-reference and modifies x?

understand.plain: This Pascal program demonstrates a classic side-effect bug. The function f takes x by reference (var x), increments it by 1, and returns the new value. Starting with x=5, the first call f(x) changes x to 6 and returns 6; the second call f(x) changes x to 7 and returns 7. So result = 6 * 7 = 42. Without the side effect (if x were passed by value), result would be 6*6=36. The key insight is that call-by-reference makes each call modify the shared variable x.

keywords.explain: In Pascal, 'var' before a parameter means the actual variable is passed, not a copy. Modifications inside the function affect the caller's variable.
keywords.example: procedure inc(var n: integer); begin n := n+1; end; — calling inc(x) changes x itself.

keywords.explain: When a function modifies a variable outside its local scope (here, the global x) as a by-product of its computation.
keywords.example: f(x) both returns a value AND changes x, so calling f(x)*f(x) is not the same as squaring one call.

keywords.explain: The order in which sub-expressions are evaluated in a compound expression like f(x)*f(x). If left-to-right: first f(x) then second f(x). Each call changes x.
keywords.example: x=5 -> f(x) returns 6 (x becomes 6) -> f(x) returns 7 (x becomes 7) -> result = 6*7 = 42.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 520 200" width="520" height="200"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="10" y="20" width="100" height="50" rx="6" class="bg"/><text x="60" y="42" class="lbl ink" text-anchor="middle">x = 5</text><text x="60" y="62" class="sub mut" text-anchor="middle">initial</text><rect x="140" y="20" width="120" height="50" rx="6" class="hi"/><text x="200" y="42" class="lbl ink" text-anchor="middle">f(x) called</text><text x="200" y="62" class="sub mut" text-anchor="middle">x: 5->6, ret 6</text><rect x="290" y="20" width="120" height="50" rx="6" class="hi"/><text x="350" y="42" class="lbl ink" text-anchor="middle">f(x) called</text><text x="350" y="62" class="sub mut" text-anchor="middle">x: 6->7, ret 7</text><rect x="180" y="110" width="160" height="55" rx="6" class="bg"/><text x="260" y="133" class="lbl ink" text-anchor="middle">result = 6 * 7</text><text x="260" y="155" class="big ok" text-anchor="middle">= 42</text><line x1="200" y1="70" x2="260" y2="110" stroke="#7F77DD" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="350" y1="70" x2="260" y2="110" stroke="#7F77DD" stroke-width="2"/></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Execution trace: x starts at 5. First f(x) call increments x to 6 and returns 6. Second f(x) call increments x to 7 and returns 7. result = 6 * 7 = 42.

given.aim: Trace the Pascal program with call-by-reference to determine the printed value of result.

terms.meaning: Global integer variable x initialized to 5
terms.connects: Starting value for both f(x) calls

terms.meaning: Function takes x by reference, increments it, and returns new value
terms.example: If x=5 before call, after call x=6 and f returns 6
terms.connects: Each call to f changes the global x, so second call sees updated x

terms.meaning: Evaluate f(x) twice and multiply results; because of call-by-reference, each call changes x
terms.example: Left-to-right: f(x)=6 (x becomes 6), then f(x)=7 (x becomes 7), product = 42
terms.connects: Side effects mean this is NOT equivalent to squaring f(x)

given.plan: Trace left-to-right evaluation: start x=5, first f(x) makes x=6 returns 6, second f(x) makes x=7 returns 7, result = 6*7 = 42.

steps.title: Initialize x and call first f(x)
steps.apply: x := 5. In expression f(x)*f(x), left operand f(x) is evaluated first (Pascal evaluates left to right). Inside f: x := x+1 = 6; f := x = 6; x is now 6 globally.
steps.note: Because x is passed by reference (var), the global x is modified from 5 to 6.

steps.title: Call second f(x)
steps.apply: Now x = 6. Second f(x) is evaluated: x := x+1 = 7; f := x = 7; x is now 7 globally.
steps.note: The second call sees x=6 (already modified by first call), not the original x=5.

steps.title: Compute result and writeln
steps.note: This demonstrates the classic side-effect problem: f(x)*f(x) != f(x)^2 when f has side effects on x. The expected answer for GATE 1998 Q9.5.2 is 42.

solution.result: The program prints 42. result = f(x) * f(x) = 6 * 7 = 42 due to call-by-reference side effects. Answer: C. 42.

questions.formula_note: Call-by-reference side effects: when a function modifies its reference parameter, repeated calls to f(x)*f(x) are not idempotent — each call sees the updated x, producing 6*7=42, not 5^2=25.
